data.title: Essential Properties: Buy-The-Dip Opportunity
data.description:
Patience is the name of the game, especially when it comes to compounded returns based on dividends and growth. Dividend stocks may not be hot conversation topics at cocktail parties, especially when compared to trendy names in cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin (BTC-USD) or AI Stocks like NVIDIA (NVDA).

However, investors who ignore dividends do so at their own peril as those income streams have a cumulative effect that materially adds to total returns. This brings me to Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), which I last covered a while back in February 2024, highlighting its quality properties and positioning in the underserved middle market.

Since then, EPRT has delivered an impressive 37% total return, far surpassing the 29% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe, as shown below.

At the current price of $30.85, EPRT trades well below its 52-week high of $34.73, particularly after the recent dip in price. It also comes with a reasonable forward P/FFO of 14.2 and a 4.0% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit EPRT including recent business results, and discuss why the recent dip creates a great buying opportunity on this growth REIT, so let's get started!

Why EPRT?

Essential Properties Realty Trust is a net lease REIT focused on single-tenant, service-oriented properties across the U.S. It primarily partners with middle-market operators through sale-leaseback transactions, providing capital while securing long-term, triple-net leases.

This niche positioning has allowed EPRT to carve out a differentiated role as a capital provider rather than a traditional landlord. This means that EPRT emphasizes granular deal sourcing and relationship-driven origination with local businesses.

This, combined with its smaller size, has translated into an outsized total return of 222% compared to net lease peers Realty Income (O), NNN REIT (NNN), W. P. Carey (WPC), and VICI Properties (VICI) over the past 10 years, as shown below.

At present, EPRT has 2,300 properties with an emphasis on high unit-level rent coverage. It gets unit-level financial reporting from 99.2% of tenants (as percentage of ABR), enabling management to keep a close tab on portfolio health. The portfolio is geared toward essential services that are e-commerce and recession-resilient.

As shown below, auto service, quick service restaurants, childcare, healthcare, and car washes make up the top 5 industries, representing 54% of ABR.

EPRT continued to deliver solid results during Q4 2025, with AFFO per share rising by 9% YoY to $0.49, landing at the high end of expectations. This was driven by stable tenant performance including rent escalator and accretive investments.

During Q4, EPRT deployed $296 million in capital at a high weighted average cash yield of 7.7%. This compares favorably to peers O and NNN, which saw initial cash yields of 7.1% to 7.3% on investments made during the same quarter.

Importantly, these acquisitions came with unit-level rent coverage of 4.7x, sitting higher than the 3.6x portfolio average. 85% of these investments were sourced through existing relationships, reflecting the value of EPRT's relationship-driven sourcing model. Moreover, occupancy remained very high at 99.7% and under 1% of EPRT's ABR is under credit watchlist, marking a sequential QoQ improvement.

Looking ahead to Q1 results and beyond, I expect to see continued strong growth through acquisitions. This is supported by management's guidance for $1 billion to $1.4 billion in acquisitions this year. Management has noted that growth is not constrained by opportunity (of which there are many), but rather by the objective of generating consistent risk-adjusted returns over time.

At present, cap rates on opportunities remain stable in the high-7% range with modest compression not expected until later this year. Notably, management has noted that competitive pressure on deals have not intensified as previously expected.

EPRT maintains a strong balance sheet with BBB-/BBB credit ratings from S&P and Fitch. This is supported by a low net debt to EBITDA ratio of 3.8x and $1.4 billion in liquidity. This gives EPRT plenty of flexibility to fund investments without heavy reliance on equity issuance.

EPRT currently yields a respectable 4%. While this isn't high compared to O and NNN, the dividend comes with a lower payout ratio of just 59%. This leaves plenty of room for internal funding of growth with retained cash flows and future dividend growth. EPRT has a 5-year dividend CAGR of 5.4% and 7 consecutive years of dividend growth.

I continue to find value in EPRT at the current price of $30.85, particularly after the recent dip in price. At this price, it has a forward P/FFO of 14.2, sitting well below its historical P/FFO of 17.3, as shown below.

EPRT's valuation sits just above O's forward P/FFO of 13.9 and above NNN's P/FFO of 12.1. While I view NNN as being the value play in the net lease REIT sector right now, EPRT is my top growth pick at a reasonable valuation.

At the present price, I don't think the market fully appreciate EPRT's high FFO/share growth potential in the 6% to 9% range. This, combined with plenty of retained cashflow and a 4% dividend yield, could result in continued total returns in the teens from here.

Risks to EPRT include those on tenant watchlist. Tenant bankruptcies could result in property vacancies and downtime, which could impact near-term results. This could be exacerbated by higher oil prices and inflation. Increased competition for deals could compress EPRT's investment yields on acquisitions. Plus, higher interest rates could raise EPRT's interest rates and cost of funding.

Investor Takeaway

The recent pullback in Essential Properties Realty Trust presents a compelling entry point for long-term income and growth investors. EPRT has solid relationship-driven acquisition growth and strong portfolio fundamentals. It also has high occupancy and a conservative balance sheet.

EPRT's lower payout ratio versus peers and consistent AFFO growth support continued dividend increases and reinvestment opportunities. As such, its 4% dividend yield positions it as a total return story rather than a pure income play.

Lastly, the recent dip in price has created an undervalued opportunity on the stock, while it trades below historical valuation. This sets up potential for total returns in the teens. For these reasons, I believe EPRT stands out as a disciplined, growth-oriented REIT capable of delivering attractive compounded returns over time.

data.title: Amcor: Dividend Aristocrat With A Great Yield And Below Fair Value
data.description:
My Investing Philosophy

I like dividend aristocrats and kings because these companies exhibit a commitment to rising dividends, and I like rising dividends. I also like to find companies that fit these definitions that are out of favor and selling at a discount to their respective fair values.

I use multiple sources of analysis and expected growth rates as well as articles, opinions and corporate data releases/presentations. But I prefer to discern from all that for myself what I really expect from a company and then plug my assumptions into my models to find what I believe to be the true fair value of a company based upon discounted future cash flows and my own required rate of return.

I like to add stocks to my portfolio of companies that I expect to have competitive advantages, good management and strong balance sheets combined with a business model that has weathered multiple periods of economic and geopolitical turmoil only to emerge stronger, demonstrating resilience. To understand my background, experience and more about my investing philosophy, check out my profile.

I believe that Amcor (AMCR) fits that description and I will use this article to explain why.

Company Background

Amcor is a packaging company, in the same industry as Sonoco (SON) which I wrote about in June of last year. At the time of that article, SON was trading at ~$44.26/share with a dividend yield of about 5%. Today that stock is trading at $54.84 and yielding 4.5%. In less than a year I have a total return of 27.5%. In that article I forecasted an average annual total return of 27.8% over the next five years. So far, so good on that one. In case you missed it, I thought I’d write about another company in the same industry with good prospects. I remain long SON.

Amcor designs and manufactures packaging for multiple industries such as food, pharmaceutical, medical and consumer products. It emphasizes lightweight, reusable and recyclable packaging. It is the outcome of a merger between Australia-based Amcor and U.S.-based Bemis in 2019.

The company closed on an acquisition of Berry Global in April 2025 and is ahead of schedule in its quest to leverage synergies to cut costs. On an adjusted basis (removing the one-time costs associated with the acquisition), both margins and EPS are showing significant improvements.

Humans will continue to buy stuff, and it will come in packages. Of that I am certain. I also believe that the benefits that can be achieved through economies of scale will be more important to the future of the packaging industry than AI. Amcor is a leader in terms of innovation and size and I believe that will enable it to capture further cost efficiencies.

I recommend reading an article by Juan Ignacio Orlando, here on Seeking Alpha, for an in-depth description of Amcor and its operations. He does an excellent job of explaining how Amcor’s size and footprint across 400 locations in 40 countries provide it competitive advantages. His article goes deeper into the operations side while I intend to focus more on the valuation.

Earnings

Amcor’s fiscal year ends in June, so the most recent quarterly earnings report on February 3 was for its second quarter. You can find the quarterly earnings presentation here.

For the quarter, net sales increased by 68% to $545 million, primarily as a result of its acquisition of Berry Global. Management’s ability to capture synergies from the combination will prove crucial to improving EPS (earnings per share). So far, the progress has been very positive.

The packaging industry is resilient but cyclical. It is best to buy when a company in this industry is out of favor and only when the company has exhibited the ability to come out of down cycles in better shape than when entering. Investor concerns over the acquisition have created just such an opportunity with Amcor.

Large, multinational companies, like PepsiCo (PEP) and McDonald’s (MCD), need packaging to be consistent in design and quality across their entire global footprint. There is an extensive process involved for one of these companies to move packaging from one company to another. First, it must find a packaging company that can serve it on a global scale, of which there are very few that can match Amcor’s global footprint. Second, it would need to find a company that can also produce the multiple types of packaging requirements for different products. Again, Amcor is well-positioned to address such issues better than smaller competitors.

Finally, economy of scale like Amcor has achieved allow it to achieve greater concessions on pricing of raw materials from suppliers than its smaller competitors, which can enable Amcor to compete on price better than many of its competitors. Size matters in this business.

The earnings history shown below displays a mostly positive picture from the EPS predictability perspective. Revenue reports have missed but continue to show mostly positive improvement on a Y/O/Y (year/over/year) basis, especially since the acquisition.

Projected consensus EPS exhibit strong growth going forward, while I believe these estimates will probably be adjusted down as the actual year-end reporting periods approach, it is important to remember that this is the game Wall Street analysts play every year; extreme optimism at the beginning with downward adjustments throughout the year to make beating estimates easier. So I think the growth rate will be lower but still very positive.

Growth Prospects

Speaking of growth, the growth grade shown in the table below shows an expectation of average growth, which once the first year of the Berry acquisition has passed, I believe will be accurate. My forecast is for long-term EPS growth of ~7% average annually over the next five years.

However, with the expanded footprint, capabilities and customers resulting from the acquisition, Amcor has some interesting growth prospects available to it, as I explained earlier in the article.

Management projects 10-15% growth in EPS, as shown in the graphic below from the Amcor quarterly earnings presentation. That may be a bit optimistic, in my opinion. But management is always presenting an optimistic view of the future, no matter which company it is.

You should note that management also projects a future dividend yield 3-4%. I assume that means that dividend growth will be slower than EPS growth rather than a dividend cut since management also reiterates its commitment to continue to grow the dividend.

Profitability

The profitability grade shown below is slightly above average (B-) and I would agree with that assessment. Two metrics I look at closely are return on total capital, which is about average for the sector, and cash from operations, which is very high relative to the sector. I like companies that can generate strong cash flows. That usually helps protect the dividend.

Management at Amcor is focused on extracting cost savings from synergies that can be identified from the acquisition. Reducing headcount in back-office operations is only one area. Eliminating less efficient facilities where those operations duplicate efforts by other, more efficient facilities is another. And cross-selling both new and existing customers with products that were not previously available to them from one of the two organizations pre-merger can create more demand and improve efficiencies by increasing capacity utilization.

The company projects total cumulative savings of $650 million through 2028. That all flows down to the bottom line, so improved EPS should be good during that period.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet currently has more debt than I would like, but management appears to be committed to using free cash flow to reduce leverage to a more reasonable level. Again, this is something to keep an eye on over the coming quarters to ensure that management is making the necessary progress.

If we assume management is up to the task, which I believe it is, then the deleveraging process should allow the company to maintain an investment grade credit rating.

Dividend

The dividend grades do not fully credit the company’s full history because of the lack of long-term history for the combined company. If we assume the dividend track record of Bemis as the basis for the dividend growth history, we find that the company has a record of 42 consecutive years of dividend growth, making it a dividend aristocrat. The current company, post-merger, has only been around for 7 years, thus the 6 years of dividend growth displayed below.

The dividend growth rate has been only 2% over the past five years, but I expect that rate to increase, although not as much as the growth rate for EPS as the company gradually brings the payout ratio down closer to 60%.

I really like the 6.5% current yield. Such a yield is hard to find among dividend aristocrats. I also like the prospects of a rising dividend. Both allow me to remain relaxed while I wait for the appreciation I expect to occur, as I’ll discuss in the valuation section later.

Valuation

I completely agree with the valuation grade below (A). By my calculations, Amcor is trading at a discount to fair market value of about 15-16%. I expect the stock to revert to its true market value over time as future reported results display the modest, but predictable growth I expect.

Let’s apply a couple of different valuation methods to see what AMCR should be worth going forward. First, if I look at AMCR through the lens of my DDM (Dividend Discount Model), I get a very favorable outcome of a $47.27 fair market value.

For the discount rate I use in this model, I substitute my required rate of return for the cost of capital. That personalizes the formula to help me identify companies that meet my criteria for the long term, rather than a valuation that is subject to change with movements in interest rates. I also vary the discount rate from one industry to another to keep my expectations realistic (I expect higher rates of return from technology than utilities, for example). AMCOR is in the materials sector, for which I require a 10% annual compound total annual return on investment. So, my formula looks like this: D/(M-X)=V or $2.60/(.10-.045)=$47.27.

Where:

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount Rate

X = Expected Future Growth of Dividend

The next step is to determine the average annual increase to the stock’s value as it returns to fair value. the Excel formula is: =((F/C)^(1/5))-1 ; or

=((47.27/39.93)^(1/5)-1 = 3.4%

Where F is the fair value and C is the current stock price.

Now we add the average annual valuation gain of 3.4% to the dividend yield of 6.5% and the expected growth in EPS of 7% to get a CAGR of 16.9%.

Another way to value AMCR is to add the dividend, expected growth in earnings, and the expected growth from the multiple expansion. This last piece requires a formula to calculate the annual compound expected growth in the PE from the current level to the historical average (reversion to the mean). Again, the Excel formula is: =((F/C)^(1/5))-1

Where:

F = Future PE ratio

C = Current PE ratio

5 = Number of years expected to revert to mean

or

=((12/10.05)^(1/5))-1=3.6%

This means that if the PE ratio rises over the next 5 years to a reasonable average of 12 from the current level of 10.05, the average annual increase in value due to PE expansion will be 3.6%.

Then we add the current dividend of 6.5% and the expected earnings growth rate of 7% to 3.6%, and we get an expected annual compound growth rate of return equal to 17.1%.

The fair value under this method is $47.64. (PE of 12 multiplied by the forward EPS of $3.97).

Finally, one other way to calculate the expected annual total return is to estimate the future EPS five years out multiplied by the expected PE of 12 to determine a target price for the stock and then add the estimated cumulative dividends we expect to collect over the five-year period. That gives us the total expected future value of our investment. Then, of course, we use another formula to determine the average expected total annual return.

Future estimated EPS in five years is calculated by assuming the average dividend growth rate of 4.5% over 5 periods, and we get an expected future dividend of $3.10 in year 5 and a cumulative total of $14.22 collected over the five-year period. Using the 7% expected growth rate for EPS, we find the expected EPS in year 5 to be $5.20/share. Multiply that by the expected PE of 12, and we get a future price target of $62.45. Then we add the future price to the cumulative dividends for a total of $76.67. That amount minus the current price (our original investment) of $39.93 results in a total gain of $36.74, or about 92%.

To calculate the compound annual total return rate we use the formula: =(($76.67/$39.93)^(1/5)-1, or 13.9%.

I like the first two results better but even at 13.9%, the investment achieves my minimum required rate of return, so I’ll take it.

Potential Risks

Of course, if the acquisition integration does not go as well as management expects, the analyst forecasts will prove to be too optimistic, and growth will not be as robust. I have tried to adjust my expectations to accommodate this risk by reducing my own projection by nearly half what the consensus is. Could even I be too optimistic? Of course, I could. Company managers are almost always overly optimistic about the acquisition they just made. I may need to temper my expectations in the future, and will do so in another article, if results do not show improvement over the next two quarters.

If the wars in Ukraine and the Middle East spread to encompass and include more nations, global economies could slow more than expected, resulting in the potential erosion of expected sales and earnings. This could also disrupt supply chains and demand.

If inflation rises due to many possible factors, not the least of which is the cost of energy, it could dampen demand globally, which could result in lower sales and earnings. It could also keep interest rates higher than expected, which could dampen multiple expansion. Again, I tried to adjust consensus expectations by lowering my future PE expectation from 15 to 12. That may not be enough in the short term, but I think it will prove conservative over the longer term.

Most of these concerns apply to almost every company in varying degrees, with the exception of the first one, which is company-specific.

Conclusion

A 14% to 17% total average compound annual return over the next five years is enticing to me and collecting the 6.5% dividend yield (and rising each year) makes the wait very comfortable. I’ll let the cash keep piling up until I find another bargain to add to my portfolio. I just love the power of compounding over time!

I rate Amcor a BUY for investors seeking current income and double-digit total returns.

data.title: TAL Education: Device And Tutoring Businesses Have Good Prospects
data.description:
I'm keeping TAL Education Group (TAL) as a "Buy"-rated name.

The stock is undervalued. I think its mid-teens earnings multiple doesn't fully reflect the growth potential of its core businesses.

TAL's bright future for the near term and over a longer horizon was detailed in my previous January 22, 2026, update.

Front-Runner In Fast-Growing Educational Hardware Space

The number of learning tablets sold in Mainland China rose 21% to 6.8 million in 2025, as per "AVC Revo/AVCR" data. The latter is a consultancy providing paid research (not publicly available) on domestic industries.

I'm encouraged that the company is a dominant player in a duopoly. AVCR's Feb 2026 statistics indicate that privately held ZuoYeBang/ZYB and TAL are the top and No. 2 sellers, representing 31.2% and 27.8% of unit sales, respectively.

Also, I like how the group is tweaking its portfolio to capitalize on demand shifts. At the 3QFY26 (YE Feb) briefing, TAL disclosed that "it launched the X5 classic learning device, positioned as a comprehensive solution in the mid-price segment."

The mid-range (CNY2-4k) learning pad category's sales contribution in the PRC went up from 2022's 21% to 2025's 63%, based on AVCR's analysis. You would assume that local consumers are prioritizing functionality over cost. The new X5's selling price is CNY3.8k, or a 29% premium over its mass-market predecessor P4.

The firm has benefited from product strategy changes and an increasingly concentrated market, in my view. It registered +120bps of share gains on a month-on-month basis in February. That's even better than the +1.1ppts achieved by ZYB for the same period.

I believe TAL's device division is at the early stages of a multi-year growth cycle. IDC's projections point to a 2024-2030 CAGR of +19% for "China’s market for children’s educational smart devices" according to a KrAsia article.

Favorable Indicators For The Tutoring Segment

In my opinion, the group is performing well in educational services.

Goldman Sachs (GS) issued a report (inaccessible in public) titled "Navigating China Internet: Top AI/Apps" on February 28. GS's data suggested that "Monthly Active Users/MAUs" for TAL's Xueersi and Peiyou mobile applications were 33% and 27% YoY higher, respectively, in Jan '26. Its listed rival, New Oriental Education's (EDU), witnessed a -9% contraction in app engagement during the same timeframe.

Separately, the firm shared at the 3QFY2026 meeting that its deferred revenue balance was USD 1,162.8 million" as of end of Nov 2025. This is equivalent to a substantial +40.8% surge on YoY terms. My Oct '25 write-up highlighted that TAL's "learning fee is collected in advance and is initially recorded as deferred revenue before being recognized proportionately."

It's reasonable for me to infer from the increase in prepayments that its tutoring services unit should grow rapidly going forward.

Another sign to watch is changing industry dynamics. A Mar 12 GS note published as "Education Tracker" mentioned that China's "offline tutoring licenses showed MoM growth for the first time since Apr 2025." My take is that this market has stabilized with the wave of exits coming to a close.

At the Q3 call, the company emphasized its "disciplined operational approach to network expansion." My bet is on TAL re-accelerating learning center openings given that the operating environment has turned favorable.

Profitability Outlook Is Positive

The analysts are forecasting that TAL's normalized net profit expansion will go from +75% for FY2025 to +159% in FY2026. The YoY growth for 9MFY26 has already reached +124%. The underlying assumption is a +6.4ppts net margin improvement for the full year. I view these predictions obtained from S&P Capital IQ as realistic.

In the previous sections, I've already drawn attention to the firm's growth potential. This should boost its future earnings via operating leverage. A big proportion of the overhead, such as headquarters expenses, doesn't vary with the topline.

I see TAL showing great determination to become leaner.

The company's "Selling & Marketing" or "S&M" costs declined 2% YoY for the most recent 3QFY26. In contrast, its S&M investments had spiked 91% in the same quarter a year ago.

TAL's overall Aug-Nov '25 OPEX-to-revenue also dropped by a significant 10ppts to 43%.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Bear-Case Scenarios

The group's pricier X5 tablet might suffer from weaker-than-expected sales. This can happen if Chinese users tighten their spending and downgrade to cheaper alternatives.

TAL may become overly aggressive in setting up new physical tutoring locations. The huge start-up costs will hurt profitability, assuming the pace of enrollments is slow.

My Closing Remarks

I'm still favoring TAL Education as an investment candidate.

Both TAL and EDU are trading at 14.5x forward P/E, as per S&P Capital IQ. The former deserves a premium for its superior app metrics outlined above.

TAL's capital appreciation potential is +61% (23.4/14.5), pegged to a fair "Price-to-Earnings Growth/PEG" of 1x. The consensus FY26-28 EPS CAGR estimate for the company is +23.4%.

I believe that the continued expansion of its device and tutoring operations will serve as a key catalyst.

data.title: BIT: Paying Out More Than It Earns (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (BIT), I stated that the fund may be an efficient way for investors to offset the volatility of the equity markets. Ironically, the equity markets did pull down from their highs shortly after. Unfortunately, BIT also experienced a sizeable pullback as the debt market continues to face headwinds. Since my last hold rating, BIT has now declined by about 5.8% and underperforms against the S&P 500. However, the fund has released an updated annual report for 2025 so I wanted to reassess the fund's overall value proposition and outlook for the year.

When I previously covered BIT, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 4.91%. Following the pullback in share price, BIT now trades at a discount to NAV of 7.05%. Referring to the red line on the chart below, we can see that BIT is now approaching the deepest discount to NAV levels experienced since the bottom of the market in 2022. For reference, BIT has traded at an average premium to NAV of 0.27% over the last five year period. However, this deeper discount is a direct reflection of the challenges that the fund is facing at this time. Therefore, I do not think investors should interpret this as an attractive time to accumulate shares.

Furthermore, BIT now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.8%. The distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which makes BIT an enticing choice for investors that want to prioritize income generation within their portfolio. However, I worry about the overall sustainable of the dividends going forward in a scenario where interest rates remain at their current levels. The fund is currently paying out more than it earns in income, which is eroding the underlying NAV. So let's start by reviewing the latest portfolio strategy that BIT implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest portfolio overview, BIT has total assets under management of $769.2M that are spread across ~1,794 different holdings. Unlike traditional equity funds, BIT chooses to specifically invest into a range of debt securities that can generate income. Therefore, the success of the fund relies on the health of the debt markets. In order to generate its earnings, BIT spreads its investments across different income-focused instruments, leaning heavily on high yield credit. The fund's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • High Yield: 51.52%
  • Securitized Products: 22.53%
  • Agency Mortgages: 15.79%
  • Investment Grade Credit: 12.48%
  • Non-US Developed: 12.44%
  • Emerging Markets: 5.58%
  • Bank Loans: 3.67%

Despite the focus on income, BIT still aims to diversify its securities across different industries. For instance, BIT is so diverse that the build of its holdings isn't labeled to any specific industry, which makes up 23.79% of assets. This is followed by varying allocations to different sectors, including communications, consumer cyclicals, and capital goods, just to name a few. This level of diversity helps to eliminate any sort of concentration risk to any one sector.

Furthermore, the fund tends to spread its allocation across varying maturity schedules. For instance, more than ~87% of total securities have a maturity date that is less than five years out. Generally, shorter-dated maturities hold up a lot better through shifting interest rates. Conversely, securities with a longer termed maturity tend to share a more obvious inverse relationship with interest rates. When interest rates rise, the value of these securities decline.

Short-dated securities don't experience as much volatility but the downside is that management has to actively rotate out of these positions and replace them with new investments to replace lost earnings. If management fails to grow its portfolio, this leads to poor earnings growth.

Performance Expectations

I want to emphasize that a position in BIT makes the most sense for investors that are prioritizing income generation above all else. The fund does not have exposure to traditional equities so it isn't structurally capable of participating in market rallies over time. The fund's decision to tie its investments to a diverse range of debt securities means that its success ultimately relies on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. So BIT is a very specific fund that I imagine would only make sense for retirees that can afford to sacrifice some total return potential.

A lot of the structural weaknesses of the fund become clear when assessing BIT's latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.93 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.13 per share. Looking over the prior years, we can see that net investment income has usually stayed within a consistent range but it has never been strong enough to support distributions on its own. The failure to generate enough income to support distributions is my main issue with the fund because it then introduces a reliance on net realized gains to make up the difference. This structural flaw is why the NAV has failed to recover back to its high of 2021. For reference, the NAV has trended lower over the last few years:

  • 2021: $17.98 per share
  • 2022: $14.66 per share
  • 2023: $14.76 per share
  • 2024: $14.50 per share
  • 2025: $13.91 per share

Since the distribution is so high, the fund is forced to sell off nearly all of its earnings every year. More importantly, the fund is frequently faced with the issue of paying out more than it actually earns, contributing to NAV erosion and causing invested capital to erode. A good demonstration of this can be seen when assessing BIT's performance over the last ten years.

For instance, BIT's share price has declined by more than 20.7% over the last decade. Despite market indices rising to new all-time highs over the last ten years, BIT wasn't able to participate. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the funds total return still sits at 106% over the same time frame. So although positive returns can be achieved over a longer holding period, this may come at the cost of your capital eroding over time.

In order to improve the fund's capacity for capital appreciation, it ultimately comes down to management's ability to grow its base assets and earnings potential. This can be achieved by investing into new debt securities, which can grow its base net investment income over time. If the fund was capable of this, the share price wouldn't experience such a drastic level of declines during periods of declines. However, the challenge remains the high interest rate environment that reduces the amount of borrowers seeking debt financing. During the Fed's last meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged.

One of the challenges is that BIT continues to utilize leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings when possible. Total effective leverage now totals 30.48% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$336.4M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this high interest rate environment. The problem is that leverage also amplifies risks in this high interest rate environment and shrinks the operating spread between the cost of BIT's debt and the yield it collects from securities. So if BIT decides to hold a potential using leverage, a downside movement amplifies losses for the fund.

Looking forward, the only relief for the fund would be for interest rates to decline further. If the federal funds rate is capable of getting back to its pre-pandemic levels, we may finally see some relief to existing positions and the environment would encourage growth. In a healthier environment, the NAV may regain some positive momentum and it may then be time to accumulate shares.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1237 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.8%. Assuming the fund keeps the payouts the same over the next twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.4844 per share. Looking over the last few years, the fund has done a great job at keeping payouts within a very consistent range. This is great for income investors seeking reliability. Specifically, retirees may find this appealing if they are using the dividends to help support lifestyle expenses.

Although there aren't any traditional dividend raises over the last few years, the consistency also means that investors can create their own dividend growth. Just to demonstrate this concept, I ran a back test of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This back test assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $100k, but that all dividends received were reinvested back into accumulating more shares. By simply remaining invested over the last few years, investors would have seen their annual dividend income more than double.

2017 Dividend Income: $9,658

2025 Dividend Income: $19,385

However, there's one problem: the dividend isn't covered by earnings. For instance, BIT reported earnings of $1.06 per share for 2025 but it paid out $1.48 per share for the same period. In fact, the fund hasn't generated sufficient earnings since 2021. With 2026 off to a rough start, I don't believe that BIT will be capable of generating earnings that can outpace the distributions. Therefore, the fund's decision to keep payouts the same will directly contribute to the erosion of the share price.

Therefore, I would argue that BIT would benefit from a distribution cut to help preserve the health of the NAV. I believe the fund would benefit from a distribution cut of 25% because it would improve operating spreads and provide the fund with the ability to use excess cash to improve new investments. Even with a 25% cut, the dividend yield would fall somewhere around 8.8%, which is still competitive amongst the high yield landscape.

Lastly, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the distributions paid YTD were supported by net investment income. This form of distribution has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in BIT would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability. However, the portion of the distributions that are not covered by net investment income are typically supported by return of capital.

Return of capital distributions can be more tax-efficient and since they are classified as income, they aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. The tradeoff is that return of capital distributions are coming directly from the fund's NAV, which can exacerbate the downside movements. So an over-reliance on return of capital can be harmful for the long-term returns of the fund and sustainability of the share price.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that BIT is a sell at this time. Despite the deep discount to NAV valuation, the fund has very limited growth potential in the near-term. As long as interest rates remain elevated, the fund may struggle to find new investments to offset the securities with short-dated maturities. Similarly, the continuous NAV decline is likely to be exacerbated by the fact that BIT continues to pay out a lot more than it earns. Although the fund has decided to keep distributions unchanged, it is very likely that the share price will continue to erode because of its structural flaws. Forward looking, I don't see improvements until interest rates substantially decline and provides some relief to existing positions and the impact of the fund's leverage.

data.title: Amphenol: Strong Growth Supported By Hyperscalers
data.description:
By Nicholas Tan, Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Amphenol (APH), we believed that the company’s growth outlook remained robust, as we forecasted the company to grow with a 5-year average growth rate of 11.4%. We further expected its Industrial segment to capitalize on its inorganic growth. We also believed its cost control strategies, such as prudent working capital management and manufacturing facilities leasing, would support its stable expense margins moving forward. We also expected that the company could continue its acquisition strategy and improve its revenue growth.

In this analysis, we cover the company again, as its revenue growth exceeded our expectations, growing 21.25% and 51.71% YoY in 2024 and 2025, respectively. We thus first analyze the reasons behind its faster-than-expected growth and whether the company could sustain this growth. We then evaluate whether its IT datacom could support its growth, as the segment has grown by 53.2% and 127.6% in 2024 and 2025. We lastly evaluate whether the company could maintain its acquisition strategy.

Higher-than-expected Growth

We first compile its revenue by end-markets to evaluate the reasons behind its higher-than-expected revenue growth.

The company’s revenue grew higher than our expectations, climbing 21.25% and 51.71% YoY in 2024 and 2025, whereas we previously forecasted the company to grow 12.9% and 12.2% for 2024 and 2025. In 2024 and 2025, the company also grew higher than its 10-year average growth rate, showing its recent strong growth. This is mainly driven by its IT Datacom segment, which grew strongly by 53.2% and 127.6% in 2024 and 2025.

Industrial

We see that its industrial segment growth (16.4%) was relatively similar to our previous projections (16.8%) for 2024. In 2024, the company also acquired Lutze US and Europe, with total annual revenue of $175 million. However, its 2025 growth was higher than our previous projections. In 2025, the company completed its acquisition of Rochester International and Lifesync with annual revenues of $100 million each. We previously forecasted its 2025 revenue as $4,242 million, which is $146 million lower than the actual results. Adding the $200 million acquisition revenue contribution to our previous projections ($4,242 million), our projections would have been in line, and so, we determined that the reason our projections were lower was due to these 2 acquisitions.

Based on our analysis, the segment’s 10-year average inorganic revenue growth of 9.6% is higher than its 10-year average organic growth of 7.5%, suggesting that the segment’s growth is more affected by inorganic growth than organic growth. Due to the large inorganic growth, we previously projected the segment using its average growth rates, as the average growth rates take into account both organic growth and inorganic growth. Since the segment’s growth rate is driven by both inorganic and organic growth, we used its average growth rate to project its growth rate, as we expect the company to continue its acquisitions in this segment and contribute to its growth rate.

Automotive

Its automotive segment growth grew by 5.4% in 2024, which was lower than our expectations of 15.1%. In its Q4 FY2024 earnings transcript, management indicated that its automotive segment was “really driven particularly by lower demand from European customers, which more than offset organic growth in North America and Asia”. According to MarkLines, Western European car sales remained flat for 2024, compared to a strong 13.9% growth in 2023. In 2025, its automotive segment growth of 13.8% was relatively in line with our growth projections of 14.1%.

For forecasting, the expected global car sales CAGR of 2.1% is much higher than the average growth rate of the segment (10.1%). However, we believe that using the average growth rate to forecast the segment is more appropriate because the segment’s growth rate benefits from its historical average inorganic growth rate of 2.5%.

Mobile Devices

Its mobile segment advanced higher than our expectations for 2024, growing 9.1% YoY. According to IDC, worldwide smartphone shipments climbed 6.4% YoY, which had likely resulted in higher-than-expected growth for this segment. However, smartphone shipments growth slowed to 2.0% in 2025 amid higher tariffs and supply chain disruption for memory chips, which we believe had lowered its mobile segment growth for 2025.

We use the mobile computing market is expected CAGR of 4.7% to forecast this segment, as its historical market growth (4.2%) is relatively in line with the market expected CAGR, and also because the segment has not completed any acquisitions for the last 3 years, which makes the market CAGR appropriate to use.

IT Datacom

In 2024 and 2025, its IT datacom segment grew by 53.2% and 127.6%, much higher than our growth projections for the segment, mainly due to the data center buildup by the top hyperscalers. Based on our analysis, we found that the top 4 hyperscalers (Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG)) increased their capex by 49.3% and 67.7% to build AI data centers for AI model training in 2024 and 2025, leading to higher-than-expected IT datacom growth, as we had not taken into account the higher-than-expected capex growth from these hyperscalers.

Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, and Alphabet buy Nvidia's (NVDA) leading-edge GPUs, whereby Amphenol is the main connector supplier for Nvidia’s GPUs’ NVLinks to transmit data. As hyperscalers’ data center capex spending affects Amphenol through Nvidia, we forecast the segment’s growth rate using the data center capex spending expected CAGR from 2026 to 2029F of 37.84%.

Communications Network (Mobile + Broadband)

Its communications network segment declined by 9.1% in 2024, whereas we previously forecasted the segment to climb 5.0% in 2024, driven by 7.2% growth in its broadband segment. This is due to a decline in its broadband and mobile network segments, whereby both segments fell 9.1% YoY. In 2024, the worldwide telecom equipment market (including broadband and mobile networks) experienced “excess inventory, challenging macro environment, and difficult 5G comparisons”, leading to a 11% decline YoY.

In 2025, the company completed its acquisition of CommScope’s Outdoor Wireless Networks (OWN) and Distributed Antenna Systems (DAS), which were expected to generate around $1.3 billion in revenue for 2025. This acquisition resulted in a staggering 152.9% YoY growth in the communications and network segment and led to the segment’s outperformance against our previous revenue projections of 4.6%. We use the average growth rate of the segment of 3.0% to forecast the segment’s growth rate, as the company has completed previous acquisitions for the segment.

Military / Defense

In 2024, its military and defense segment grew by 21.3%, higher than our growth expectations of 11.5% and the market CAGR of 5.0%. During the year, the company acquired Carlisle Interconnect Technologies (CIT), which was anticipated to generate around $900 million for the year, with its products focused on the aerospace, defense, and industrial segments of the company.

In 2025, its military and defense segment improved by 24.1%, as the company acquired Trexon ($1 billion), which focused primarily on manufacturing interconnects and cables for the defense end market. Trexon’s 2025 revenue was anticipated to be $290 million, which resulted in the higher-than-expected growth from the segment. They also acquired Narda with estimated revenues of $120 million, leading to higher growth. We used the historical average growth of 14.5% to forecast the segment, as the expected defense market CAGR (5.0%) is much lower than historical growth, and also to account for the inorganic growth.

Commercial Aerospace

Its commercial aerospace climbed by 81.9% in 2024, much higher than our revenue growth projections of 9.9%. As we indicated above, the company acquired CTI from Carlisle, which was expected to generate $900 million in 2024, for those 3 end markets, which included commercial aerospace.

In 2025, its commercial aerospace grew by 26.4% YoY, higher than our growth projections of 8.9%. This was mainly due to its acquisition of Trexon. While Trexon mainly focused on the defense end market, the company also served the aerospace end market, which resulted in higher-than-expected growth for the segment. We used the historical average growth of 18.8% to forecast the segment, as the expected defense market CAGR (7.7%) is much lower than historical growth, and to account for inorganic growth for the segment.

We expect the company’s growth to continue to be driven by both inorganic and organic growth. Most of its segments achieved higher growth than the expected market CAGR for the respective segments, except for Automotive, which suggests most of its segments are more competitive than their competitors. We believe the higher competitiveness for its segments can bode well for its growth outlook. We also believe that the company will continue with its acquisition strategy, particularly in the Industrial segment, which would improve its overall growth outlook. Most of its segments achieved higher 2025 revenues than our 2025F projections, showing strong growth overall, except for Automotive, where Automotive’s 2025 revenues were lower than our previous projections. Even so, the Automotive segment growth achieved 13.8%, which is still double-digit growth, indicating that growth is still strong.

IT Datacom Growth

After evaluating its segment growth, we assess whether its IT datacom segment could support its growth outlook, and thus compile its IT datacom historical revenue.

IT datacom has been growing with an average growth rate of 29.5% over the last 10 years, rising from $891 million in 2015 to $8,314 million in 2025. Historically, its growth has been strong, driven by the increasing buildup of data centers, which require Amphenol’s connectors to connect and transmit data for their servers, networking equipment, and cloud storage. Data centers use Ethernet switches to transmit data between servers and other devices. As Ethernet switches data transfer speeds grow from 100G to 800G, this increases the demand for connectors to reliably transfer large amounts of data across servers and other hardware.

In 2024 and 2025, its IT datacom segment grew by 53.2% and 127.6%, much higher than our growth projections. As we indicated above, the top 4 hyperscalers (Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Alphabet) increased their capex by 49.3% and 67.7% in 2024 and 2025, to build AI data centers for AI model training, leading to higher-than-expected growth for the segment. This is because the top hyperscalers buy Nvidia's leading-edge GPUs, whereby Amphenol provides connectors in the GPUs’ NVLinks to transmit data inside the server racks.

For newer AI models with higher parameter counts, the computing resources required to train these AI models increase. We can see that Nvidia’s GPU FP16 performance improves with each iteration of newer GPUs. To further improve computing power, Nvidia stacks GPUs together into a server rack (GB200), where Amphenol connectors (Paladin) physically link the GPUs to NVSwitches. The NVSwitches then route the data to other GPUs using NVLink ports. NVLink ports enable GPUs to improve data transfer rates between each other to increase the amount of data supplied for better performance. As NVLink data transfer rates grow with each generation, from 900GB per second in 2022 (H100) to 1.8TB per second in 2024 and 2025 (B100 and B200), a 900GB per second increase or 100% increase.

This requires a higher number of Amphenol connectors (Paladin) to connect the GPUs with NVLink ports, whereby Amphenol is the “primary supplier” for Nvidia’s GB200 servers. For example, its Paladin HD 224 GB per second provides the high data transfer speed needed for GPUs to transfer data with each other. One reason that Amphenol is the “primary supplier” of connectors for Nvidia’s GB200 servers could be its partnership with Nvidia’s NVLink Fusion. The initiative enables hyperscalers and CSPs to create their own custom server design by integrating 3rd party CPUs and hardware into the Grace Blackwell ecosystem, while using Amphenol’s connectors in their design blueprint. This positions Amphenol’s connectors better than other competitors to be chosen as the key connector supplier, as its connectors are used in hyperscalers' design blueprint.

The company also announced its Paladin HD 224 GB per second in February 2024, which we believe was also a key contributor towards the higher-than-expected growth in 2025, as the newer connection provides double the transfer speed from its previous Paladin HD 112 GB per second released in December 2022. As such, we believe the great demand for Nvidia’s newer GPUs with higher data transfer rates from CSPs resulted in a large growth in its IT Datacom, leading to higher-than-expected growth for the segment. The telecom and datacom connector is also expected to grow with 14.1% CAGR, which should support its growth outlook.

As Nvidia’s NVLinks data transfer rates rise from 1.8 TB per second in 2025 (B300) to 3.6 TB per second in 2026 and 2027F (R100 and Rubin Ultra), we expect this to support its growth outlook further. According to MooMoo, referencing Barrons, they highlighted that “Amphenol already provides a significant share of the content in Nvidia's Blackwell platform, and Evercore sees this share increasing by 20% to 40% for Rubin”, which we believe bolsters its growth outlook for its connectors and highlights that the company continues to be a supplier for Nvidia’s upcoming GPUs.

We determine that as hyperscalers build out their AI data centers, this would increase the purchase of Nvidia GPU server racks, leading to higher demand for Amphenol’s connectors in Nvidia GPUs. We thus expect data center capex to support Amphenol’s IT datacom growth outlook. We also expect continuous improvement in NVLink transfer rates to further support its growth outlook, as this increases the reliance on Amphenol’s connectors. We further expect Amphenol to be the main supplier for Nvidia’s NVLinks due to their partnership with the NVLink Fusion. As hyperscalers’ data center capex spending affects Amphenol through Nvidia, we forecast the IT datacom’s growth rate using the data center capex spending expected CAGR from 2026 to 2029F of 37.84%.

Recently, the company has completed its acquisition of the Connectivity and Cable Solutions (CCS) business from CommScope, which would increase the breadth of interconnector products for AI and data center applications and improve its competitiveness. The CCS business is also expected to generate around $4.1 billion in 2026, towards 3 segments (IT datacom, communications network, and industrial). For the acquisition impact of CCS, we split the expected revenue across IT datacom, communications network, and industrial, as we were unable to find its specific revenue breakdown and estimated their contribution conservatively.

Acquisition Analysis

After evaluating that its IT datacom could support its growth outlook, we assess whether its acquisition strategy could further improve its segment’s growth outlook.

In 2024, its revenue grew by $2,668 million or 21.3% YoY, while acquisition revenue grew by $1,036 million or 8.3% YoY. This highlights that inorganic growth has contributed to the higher-than-expected growth of the company, as we previously forecasted the company to grow by 12.9%. Without the additional 8.3% growth from acquisitions, the company would have grown 13.0%, which is relatively inline with our forecasts. In 2025, this can also be seen, as the company grew by 51.7%, but without the acquisition growth (13.7%), the company would have grown by 38.0%. We thus believe that acquisition revenue has increased their recent growth and is the main reason for the higher-than-expected growth.

We compile its cash flow analysis to further analyze its acquisition strategy.

Its FCF (investing) margins have fluctuated significantly, much more than its FCF (CAPEX) margins, reflecting its acquisition strategy over the last 10 years. The gap between FCF (investing) margins and FCF (CAPEX) margins has also widened in 2024 and 2025, showing the large acquisition the company has recently undertaken. In 2024 and 2025, the company completed 7 acquisitions, with cash acquisitions totaling $5,975 million, leading to a large decline in FCF (investing) margins and worsening its FCFs.

Comparing its acquisitions against end market segments, its Industrial segment’s growth benefited significantly from its acquisitions. In 2024, they acquired Carlisle Interconnect Technologies (CIT) with $900 million in revenue. Based on the investor presentation in August 2022, the CIT breakdown included Aerospace (46%), Medical (35%), Defense & Space (10%), and Industrial, Test & Measurement (9%). Multiplying the $900 million in revenue against the total of Medical and Industrial (44%), we estimate this contributed around $396 million in revenue, or 10.8% of its Industrial 2024 revenue. The CIT acquisition is also complementary to its existing solutions, improving its competitiveness. The company also acquired Lutze with an anticipated $175 million in revenue. In 2025, the company also acquired Rochester and LifeSync with $100 million in revenue each, to improve its breadth of liquid sensors and interconnect products for the industrial end-market.

Its Communications Network’s segment growth has also benefited from acquisitions, as the company acquired OWN and DAS from CommScope with an expected $1.3 billion in revenue and added a “strong portfolio of innovative and advanced technologies for communications networks”.

Its Defense segment’s revenue growth also benefited from its acquisitions, as the CIT acquisition’s end markets also included defense, where we estimate CIT contributed around $90 million in 2024 revenue. In 2025, the company acquired Narda with $120 million in 2025 revenue, to improve its breadth of RF interconnect components and also to “complement” its defense solutions. In 2025, they acquired Trexon with an estimated revenue of $290 million. The company also highlighted that “high-reliability cable assembly products will be highly complementary” for its defense solutions.

Its Commercial Aerospace growth also benefited from its acquisitions, as the company acquired CTI, whereby the CTI end-market included around 46% from the aerospace end-market. Multiplying CTI’s 2024 expected revenue of $900 million against 46%, we estimate this resulted in $414 million in inorganic revenue for the segment and accounted for 45.3% of its 2024 revenue, highlighting the large impact of acquisition revenue on total commercial aerospace revenue. In 2025, the company also acquired Trexon, and while Trexon mainly focused on the defense end market, the company also served the aerospace end market.

We overall determine that these 4 segments benefited the most from its recent acquisitions (2024 to 2025), whereas IT datacom and mobile devices did not benefit from the acquisitions. For automotive, there were no notable acquisitions that had impacted its growth.

We further compile its net debt to analyze whether the company could continue acquiring companies.

The company’s debt has been slowly rising from 2016 to 2025, with a significant increase in debt in 2025, primarily reflecting the large debt underwritten to acquire CommScope's Connectivity and Cable (CCS) business for $10.5 billion in cash, with an anticipated 2026 revenue of $4.1 billion. Its cash-to-debt ratio is improving, highlighting that it is in a better financial position to undertake future acquisitions. Its 2025 cash of $11,434 million is also much higher than its historical average cost per acquisition of $290 million and yearly average cash acquisition of $1,218 million. This means that if the company continues to acquire companies based on its historical averages, its current cash position is more than sufficient for it to continue acquiring companies in that manner.

We overall determine that its FCF margins have worsened in 2024 and 2025, due to the large acquisitions undertaken. However, we believe that its previous acquisitions have improved its competitiveness, as their products are complementary to its existing solutions. As such, we expect its acquisition strategy to improve its segments' competitiveness and their growth outlook. We also find that the company’s current cash-to-debt and cash position is strong, enabling the company to continue acquiring companies if it continues to acquire companies based on its historical averages.

Risk: US Trade Tariffs

We believe one of the key risks that could impact the company is US trade tariffs. Based on its annual report, the company generates around 15.9% from China, which represents the 2nd largest market. Recently, the US Supreme Court overturned the US President’s previous tariffs and ruled that the US President has “exceeded his authority”. In response to this, the US President introduced another 150-day global 10% tariff before requiring approval from Congress, which could increase to 15%. Despite the revocation of tariffs, China’s weighted average tariffs stand at 27.2%, much higher than the average 12.1%, which we believe would negatively impact China’s geographic segment growth outlook.

Valuation

For Industrial, Automotive, Communications Network, Military & Defense, Commercial Aerospace, we project these segments to grow alongside their historical 10-year averages, tapering them down to discount future uncertainty, such as unexpected competitive developments. We used historical averages for these segments, as these segments’ growth has previously been affected by inorganic growth, and for some segments (Industrial, Communications Network, Military & Defense, Commercial Aerospace), their recent growth has been significantly impacted by acquisitions.

We forecast Mobile Devices using the expected market CAGR, as its 2024 and 2025 growth has been relatively in line with the market CAGR, and there have not been any notable acquisitions for the segment. As indicated above, we lastly forecast its IT Datacom segment growth rate using the data center capex spending expected CAGR from 2026 to 2029F of 37.84%. For the acquisition impact of CCS, we split the expected revenue across IT datacom, communications network, and industrial, as we were unable to find its specific revenue breakdown and estimated their contribution conservatively.

Based on our valuation of the company using a DCF model, incorporating our revenue projections at a 5-year forward average growth of around 21.8%, a discount rate of 10.8% reflecting the company’s WACC, and a terminal value based on the Electronic Components sub-industry average EV/EBITDA multiple of 12.45x, our model indicates a downside of 0.30%. Our growth projections for 2026F of 38.95% are slightly lower than management’s guidance of 44% growth YoY for Q1 FY2026, as we forecast a moderation of growth in 2026F compared to 2025.

Verdict

We overall determine that its growth outlook remains robust, driven by its IT datacom segment. We expect the AI data center buildup to increase demand for Nvidia’s GPUs, which would support its growth outlook, as its connectors are crucial for connecting GPUs with NVLink ports. As NVLink’s data transfer rates double in 2026 and 2027F, we believe this would bolster its growth outlook. We further expect the company to maintain its position as the primary connector supplier to Nvidia’s server racks due to its NVLink partnership. We expect other segments to also support its growth outlook. We further believe that their previous acquisitions have improved their segment’s competitiveness, due to complementary products, and that they can sustain their acquisition strategy.

Based on our DCF valuation, we thus rate Amphenol as a Hold with a price target of $122.76, higher than our previous price target of $70.49 (adjusted for a 2-for-1 stock split). This is mainly because we forecast a higher 5-year forward average growth of 21.9% than our previous 11.5%, whereby the higher forward growth can be mainly attributable to higher forward IT Datacom growth. In our previous analysis, we forecasted IT Datacom using its historical average (14.2%) and also tapered IT Datacom growth down. However, we had not considered the large capex spending by the top 4 hyperscalers for data centers, which increased their capex by 49.3% and 67.7% in 2024 and 2025, leading to higher-than-expected growth for IT Datacom. Taking into account the higher capex spending by top hyperscalers and the newer IT Datacom acquisition, we now project IT Datacom to grow with a 3-year average forward growth of 43.3%, compared to 13.2% previously, resulting in a higher forward growth.

data.title: Elastic: AI Resistant, Strong Growth, And Great Value
data.description:
As all attention in the stock market is centered around the possible escalation of tensions in Iran, investors have retained the risk-off attitude that has dominated the stock market this year, in particular hitting small- and mid-cap growth stocks hard. The "SaaSpocalypse" narrative continues to weigh heavily on investors' minds, dragging high-quality businesses down to multi-year lows.

Elastic (ESTC), the enterprise search company, is one of these stocks. Already down ~30% since the start of the year, Elastic has nevertheless driven very consistent fundamental performance that doesn't suggest AI is a risk at all for its business (perhaps it's actually even a tailwind, thanks to the explosion of app-building that it's enabling).

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Elastic in November, when the stock was trading at $82 per share. Since then, Elastic has seen a huge erosion in value. Needless to say, I acknowledge that my prior "Buy" call was ill-timed and that I didn't correctly predict the SaaSpocalypse backlash that would build against SaaS stocks in 2026. That said, selloffs of this magnitude require a fresh look, and I find very few red flags in Elastic that warrant this selloff. I reiterate my "Buy" rating here.

Over the last few months, investors have focused too heavily on the unproven possibility that AI will begin to decimate software stocks. In my view, there are many tailwinds to the Elastic bull case that are being overlooked, which are:

  • Elastic has long deployed a consumption-based pricing model, which insulates the company from AI-driven layoffs. Part of the panic in the software industry is that AI will drive layoffs, particularly in technical/IT teams that use products like Atlassian (TEAM) and ServiceNow (NOW). Elastic has never deployed a seat-based pricing model, as its business is centered around data consumption - which is what many software companies are trying to switch to right now.
  • High net retention rates. As another feature of Elastic's consumption-based pricing model, the company enjoys net retention rates of 112% in the most recent quarter, indicating that customers continue to spend increasingly on the Elastic platform.
  • AI tailwinds. Elastic has a complex product that integrates data signals from various applications to allow developers to build contextual search features in their applications. This isn't a product that can be easily ripped out and replaced with AI. On the other hand, the app-building boom that AI has kicked off may actually be spurring more need for Elasticsearch.
  • Growth/profitability balance. Elastic combines a high-teens revenue growth rate alongside high-teens pro forma operating margins, positioning the stock just under the Rule of 40.

By far the most compelling reason to invest in the stock now, however, is an undemanding valuation. At current share prices near $51, Elastic trades at a $5.24 billion market cap. Netting off the $1.25 billion of cash and $570.6 million of debt on Elastic's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $4.56 billion.

For FY27 (the upcoming fiscal year for Elastic ending in April 2027), Wall Street analysts are expecting revenue to hit $1.97 billion, or 13% y/y growth. This positions Elastic's valuation at just 2.3x EV/FY27 revenue.

It comes as no surprise to anybody now that enterprise software stocks have taken deep cuts in valuation. At the same time, even among a battered sector, Elastic's ~2x multiples sit on the low end compared to other companies that are expected to grow in the ~teens.

Stay long here and use Elastic's dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Elastic's fiscal Q3 (January quarter) results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Elastic's revenue grew 18% y/y to $449.9 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $438.4 million (+15% y/y) by a three-point margin, and also accelerating two points versus 16% y/y growth in Q2 (though we note that FX moments and the weakening dollar also contributed to 2 points of y/y growth within the quarter).

What we're looking for specifically in Elastic's results is for cleaner evidence that the company is capable of withstanding any supposed headwinds from AI, and in Elastic we find several such indicators. For one, Elastic has recently stepped up its acquisition of large customers. Its count of customers who generate more than >$100k in ACV (annual contract value) increased by 60 customers sequentially to 1,660, up 14% y/y.

As a reminder, Elastic's base of large blue-chip customers spans several important industries. Key Elastic clients include the likes of Home Depot (HD), Ford (F), Wells Fargo (WFC), Adobe (ADBE), Comcast (CMCSA), Pfizer (PFE), and Uber (UBER). The fact that the company continues to see its base of large customers expanding is a signal that IT departments don't see an easy way to replace Elastic's functions.

As another telling indicator of this durability, we note that Elastic's total remaining performance obligations (RPO, which is essentially backlog) grew 22% y/y to $1.65 billion. Again, we doubt that large customers would be signing large-multi-year contracts with Elastic if they had any intention of replacing it with AI in the near term.

Management remains convinced that AI, and the app-building that it's unleashing, is a powerful tailwind and not a headwind for the company. Per CEO Ash Kulkarni's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

This data is massive, often many petabytes in scale and simply cannot be moved for cost and security reasons outside of the organization's control. For businesses to use agentic AI, the LLM needs to come to the data. This is where Elastic comes in with our ability to help organizations store and manage all of their data in very cost-effective ways and by providing accurate real-time context to AI by searching through all of this organizational data in real time [...]

And the results are clear. The number of commitments for over $1 million in annual commitment value signed this quarter grew over 30% compared to the same period last year, driven by new logos and customer expansion. Consolidation and AI are powerful tailwinds. As organizations manage exploding data volumes, they are turning to Elastic to drive both innovation and efficiency to their search, observability and security needs.

Meanwhile, the company also continued to step up profitability. Pro forma operating margins rose 180bps y/y to 18.6%, which also represented 210bps of sequential improvement from 16.5% in Q2 (in which operating margins had temporarily declined by less than a point).

We see the company continuing to generate economies of scale, especially as net retention trends continue to boost average customer spending.

Key takeaways

With consistent growth trends, a buildup of backlog, and an expanding base of large blue-chip customers, Elastic continues to execute well fundamentally even if its stock is in a bear market. Take advantage of the disconnect between share price performance and actual strong results to "Buy" this stock.

data.title: Fortinet's Competitive Moat Is Being Tested
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Fortinet (FTNT) is one of the largest pure-play cybersecurity companies in the market and maintains a strategic model that differentiates it from its industry peers. While many competitors rely heavily on stock-based compensation and significant capital reinvestment to fuel expansion, Fortinet has established a consistent track record of GAAP profitability and market-leading capital efficiency while maintaining double-digit revenue growth. Although current market trends favor a capital-light and a digital-first approach, Fortinet's legacy hardware strategy may serve as a significant advantage during the integration of AI into the enterprise environment.

Fortinet leads its peers in GAAP profitability and capital efficiency while maintaining a horizontally integrated, hardware-based moat. However, the accelerating industry shift toward cloud-native architectures creates significant long-term uncertainty for their business model. I am initiating a Hold rating on FTNT, reflecting my cautious outlook between the company’s operational excellence and the risk of market displacement by more agile competitors.

Fortinet's Business Segment Breakdown

The cybersecurity industry is very fragmented, with numerous vendors offering niche products and services for specific needs. Because of this, I believe it is essential to look at how Fortinet’s segments actually fit together and how they fit into the broader cybersecurity landscape.

The business starts with its secure networking hardware, centered on the FortiGate firewall. These devices rely on custom-designed ASIC chips that allow for a more powerful and cost-effective product, primarily because they handle security data and processes much more efficiently than the general-purpose processors used by most competitors. Along with these firewalls, Fortinet sells their specialized FortiSwitch and FortiAP, which allow an entire office to be secured through a local network. These products currently represent about 33% of total revenue and provide the necessary physical "lock-in" for the rest of the ecosystem.

Once that hardware is in place, Fortinet can cross-sell customers into their high-margin and recurring service subscriptions, which now account for nearly 67% of total sales. This is driven by FortiGuard, an AI-powered threat intelligence service that analyzes security events every day from millions of global sensors to protect against cyberthreats like ransomware and phishing, allowing the system to automatically block malicious traffic. This is backed by FortiCare, which provides technical and software support to maintain the physical equipment and ensure the entire security network remains operational.

Beyond these core segments, the company is also aggressively investing in Unified SASE and Security Operations, both of which constitute a smaller portion of revenue but are growing at over 20% YoY. Unified SASE allows a company to protect remote workers by routing their traffic through a Fortinet cloud location, while the security operations tools help analysts automate their response to hacks. This approach allows Fortinet to provide cloud-native solutions for organizations prioritizing flexibility and lower capital investments.

Competitive Landscape

When it comes to competition, Fortinet is the leader in the secure networking segment by customer and product volume, supported by a massive base of over 900,000 customers and more than 50% of the global firewall units being shipped by Fortinet. Their primary competitive edge is the custom ASIC chip design, mentioned previously in the article. This technical advantage provides one of the highest performance-per-dollar in the industry, making Fortinet an optimal choice for large-scale data operations and for cost-conscious small-to-mid-sized businesses. However, Palo Alto Networks remains the revenue leader by focusing on premium-priced solutions and targeting larger global enterprises.

In the unified SASE market, Palo Alto Networks and Netskope are currently the established leaders, but Fortinet’s ability to offer a unified platform that works seamlessly across both cloud and hardware is a growing threat to their dominance. Palo Alto’s edge in this segment comes from a fully integrated, AI-powered platform that is recognized as a leader across nearly every major security category, according to Gartner. While Fortinet is a top competitor here, newer customers currently favor the simplified management experience offered by cloud-native platforms such as Cloudflare or Zscaler.

Microsoft remains a dominant leader in the Security Operations (SecOps) segment, followed by Fortinet, Palo Alto Networks, Google Cloud, and Crowdstrike. Microsoft’s competitive advantage is built on its massive data footprint through its ownership of the Windows operating system and the Office 365 productivity suite. This value proposition allows their tools to detect and respond to threats across a company's entire software environment more efficiently than a third-party cybersecurity provider.

Overall, Palo Alto Networks is Fortinet's most significant competitor, as both companies are aggressively pursuing a platformization product strategy. This approach involves embedding their physical or cloud infrastructure as the initial point of entry and then expanding their footprint by cross-selling a full suite of integrated cybersecurity services. While Palo Alto captures the premium enterprise market, Fortinet’s scale and efficiency make it the primary challenger for organizations that prioritize high performance at a lower cost.

To illustrate the competitive landscape, the chart below outlines key financial metrics and revenue growth expectations for Fortinet and its industry peers.

Ticker Market Cap Revenue P/S Ratio GAAP EPS

FWD P/E Ratio (Non-GAAP)

 NTM Revenue Growth
FTNT $61B $6.8B 9.1x $2.42 27.6x 14.2%
PANW $130.8B $9.9B 11.2x $1.80 43.9x 15.4%
CRWD $99B $4.5B 20.8x ($0.65) 82x 21.7%
NET $72.6B $2.2B 34x ($0.29) 188.8x 29.8%
ZS $22.6B $3B 7.3x ($0.43) 34.65x 23.9%
CHKP $15B $2.7B 5.8x $9.62 14.19x 6.25%

The Infrastructure Moat

There is a growing disconnect in how the market currently values digital (software) moats versus physical (infrastructure) moats. This is largely because the long-term impact of AI on software remains difficult to calculate, while the staying power of a physical layer is much easier to conceptualize. For a business, it is far simpler to let a software license expire or spend a few weeks retraining a workforce on a new AI-generated tool than it is to rip out an entire data center of firewalls that an organization’s cybersecurity system depends on. Consequently, once a company commits capital to Fortinet's hardware, this creates a barrier to exit, which encourages customer retention. This hardware-based moat provides Fortinet with substantial resilience, particularly if AI-driven shifts begin to challenge competitors that rely exclusively on a digital presence.

Additionally, as AI agents become a core part of the enterprise workforce, they will likely be managed by smaller teams prioritizing streamlined operations. To achieve this efficiency, these agents must access data from internal, on-premise systems rather than through public cloud networks. Furthermore, because cybersecurity breaches are now a common occurrence, the compromise of one of these AI agents is a high-likelihood threat that could be detrimental to a company’s proprietary data and operational stability. All of which further incentivizes the need for a sovereign data environment and a localized security system, which cannot be achieved by a cloud-only approach. Fortinet’s physical hardware provides a secure environment for these internal agents to operate within internal data centers, distinguishing the company from cloud-native competitors like Zscaler or CrowdStrike.

Fortinet's Financial Strength and Management Efficiency

Fortinet stands out in the cybersecurity sector because of its unique financial discipline and long-term management strategy. While most high-growth competitors rely on high levels of stock-based compensation that can dilute the value for regular shareholders, Fortinet has maintained consistent GAAP profitability with a much lower reliance on these practices. Rather than using capital for aggressive acquisitions to expand its product line, management has historically prioritized different activities, such as share buybacks. This "shareholder-friendly" approach is a significant differentiator compared to companies like Zscaler or CrowdStrike, which have often favored rapid expansion and market share over profitability and capital efficiency.
